---
title: Nginx Proxy Manager
description: Reverse proxy with Let's Encrypt SSL via a clean web UI. No Nginx config required.
---

import Mermaid from '../../../components/Mermaid.astro';

**Image:** `jc21/nginx-proxy-manager:2.11.3`  
**Ports:** `80` (HTTP), `443` (HTTPS), `81` (admin UI)  
**Data:** `npm_data` (proxy config), `npm_letsencrypt` (certificates)

## Introduction

[Nginx Proxy Manager](https://nginxproxymanager.com/) provides a web UI for managing Nginx as a reverse proxy. It handles SSL certificate issuance and renewal via Let's Encrypt automatically — no Nginx configuration files required.

This template **owns** the `proxy-net` shared bridge network. It must be started before any service that opts into that network.

## When to Use

- You want HTTPS for your self-hosted services without writing Nginx config
- You need automatic Let's Encrypt certificate management
- You want to route multiple domain names to services running on the same host

## Architecture

<Mermaid diagram={`
graph LR
    Internet["Internet"] -->|":80 HTTP"| NPM
    Internet -->|":443 HTTPS\nTLS terminated"| NPM
    You["Admin Browser"] -->|":81 Admin UI"| NPM

    subgraph NPM["Nginx Proxy Manager"]
        UI["Admin UI"]
        Nginx["Nginx reverse proxy"]
        npm_data["npm_data volume\n(config)"]
        npm_le["npm_letsencrypt volume\n(certificates)"]
    end

    NPM -->|"proxy-net\ncontainer name"| SQ["SonarQube"]
    NPM -->|"proxy-net\ncontainer name"| Jenkins["Jenkins"]
    NPM -->|"proxy-net\ncontainer name"| Other["Other opted-in\nservices"]
    NPM <-->|"ACME challenge"| LetsEncrypt["Let's Encrypt\n(internet)"]
`} />

## Prerequisites

- Docker Engine 20.10+, Docker Compose v2
- Ports **80 and 443 must be free** on the host — nothing else can bind these ports
- For Let's Encrypt: a public domain name pointing to your host's IP

## Ports

| Port | Protocol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `80` | HTTP | Public HTTP (used for ACME HTTP-01 challenge) |
| `443` | HTTPS | Public HTTPS (TLS-terminated reverse proxy) |
| `81` | HTTP | Admin web UI (do not expose to internet) |

## Volumes

| Volume | Contents |
|---|---|
| `npm_data` | Proxy host configs, user database, settings |
| `npm_letsencrypt` | SSL certificates and ACME account keys |

## Configuration

```bash
cd nginx-proxy-manager
cp .env.example .env
```

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `NPM_HTTP_PORT` | `80` | HTTP port — must be 80 for Let's Encrypt HTTP-01 |
| `NPM_HTTPS_PORT` | `443` | HTTPS port |
| `NPM_ADMIN_PORT` | `81` | Admin UI port |

## Workflow

```bash
# Create the shared network first (owned by this template)
docker network create proxy-net

# Start NPM
docker compose up -d

# Open admin UI
open http://localhost:81

# Default credentials (MUST change on first login):
# Email:    admin@example.com
# Password: changeme
```

**Adding a proxy host:**
1. Login to admin UI → **Proxy Hosts → Add Proxy Host**
2. Set **Domain Names** to your domain (e.g., `jenkins.yourdomain.com`)
3. Set **Scheme** to `http`, **Forward Hostname** to the container name (e.g., `jenkins`), **Forward Port** to the internal port (e.g., `8080`)
4. On the **SSL** tab, request a Let's Encrypt certificate

**Opting a service into proxy-net:**
1. Open the service's `docker-compose.yml`
2. Uncomment the NPM block at the bottom (see [Networking](/networking/#the-npm-opt-in-pattern))
3. `docker compose up -d` in that service's directory
4. The container name is now resolvable from NPM

**Stopping NPM:**
```bash
docker compose down

# Stop and remove all data (proxy hosts, SSL certificates) — destructive
docker compose down -v
```

## Use Cases

**HTTPS for [Jenkins](/templates/jenkins/):** Domain `jenkins.yourdomain.com` → `jenkins:8080`  
**HTTPS for [SonarQube](/templates/sonarqube/):** Domain `sonar.yourdomain.com` → `sonarqube:9000`  
**HTTPS for [Portainer](/templates/portainer/):** Domain `portainer.yourdomain.com` → `portainer:9000` (or use Portainer's native HTTPS on 9443 directly)  
**HTTPS for [Homarr](/templates/homarr/):** Domain `home.yourdomain.com` → `homarr:7575`

## Security

:::danger[Change default credentials]
Default credentials (`admin@example.com` / `changeme`) must be changed immediately on first login. Anyone who can reach port 81 can take over all your proxy configurations.
:::

:::caution[Admin UI exposure]
Do not expose port 81 to the internet. Restrict it to localhost or a trusted network only. If you need remote admin access, VPN in or restrict it with firewall rules.
:::

## Docker Desktop Notes

On Docker Desktop, ports 80 and 443 should be available. If another process already binds these ports (e.g., a local web server), stop it first or remap NPM to alternate ports (though Let's Encrypt HTTP-01 requires port 80).
